sentences.base_language: Excuse me, where is the station?
sentences.grammarNote:
**Disculpe** is the formal (usted) imperative of **disculpar** — use it when approaching a stranger.
**¿Dónde está...?** = Where is...? Location always uses **estar**, never **ser**.
**La estación** — feminine noun (all **-ción** nouns are feminine).

sentences.base_language: Go straight ahead and turn left at the intersection.
sentences.target_language: Vaya todo recto y gire a la izquierda en el cruce.
sentences.grammarNote: Formal (usted) commands use the present subjunctive: vaya (ir), gire (girar), siga (seguir), cruce (cruzar). Informal (tú) commands are different: ve, gira, sigue, cruza. Use usted with strangers you've just approached.

sentences.base_language: Cross the bridge and the station is on your right.
sentences.target_language: Cruce el puente y la estación está a su derecha.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Cruce** is the formal (usted) imperative of **cruzar** (to cross).
**A su derecha** = on your right (formal **su** = your). Informal: **a tu derecha**.
**A la derecha** (to/on the right) · **a la izquierda** (to/on the left) — always use **a + la** because **derecha** and **izquierda** are feminine nouns.

**¿A qué distancia está?** = How far is it? Literally 'At what distance is it?'
**Está** (not **es**) — distance/location uses **estar**.
Alternative: **¿Está lejos?** (Is it far?) or **¿Cuánto hay de aquí?** (How far from here?).

sentences.base_language: It's about five minutes on foot.
sentences.target_language: Está a unos cinco minutos a pie.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Está a + time/distance** = It's [time/distance] away: **está a cinco minutos** (5 minutes away), **está a dos kilómetros** (2 km away).
**Unos** = about/approximately (masculine plural of **un**): **unos cinco minutos**, **unas tres horas**.
**A pie** = on foot. Compare transport phrases: **en coche** (by car), **en autobús** (by bus), **en metro** (by metro).

sentences.base_language: Is there a bus stop near here?
sentences.target_language: ¿Hay una parada de autobús por aquí?
sentences.grammarNote:
**¿Hay...?** = Is there...? **Hay** (from **haber**) is used for existence — never **está** for introducing something new/indefinite.
**Por aquí** = around here / in this area. **Por** here indicates approximate location in a zone.
**La parada de autobús** = the bus stop. **La parada** (feminine) = the stop/halt.

sentences.base_language: Yes, there's one next to the bank.
sentences.target_language: Sí, hay una al lado del banco.
sentences.grammarNote: 'Del' is the mandatory contraction of 'de + el'. 'Al lado del banco' (next to the bank). Similarly, 'al' is the contraction of 'a + el'. Both are obligatory — you can never write 'de el' or 'a el'.

**Muchas gracias** = thank you very much. **Muchas** is the feminine plural form agreeing with **gracias** (feminine plural noun).
Responses: **De nada** (you're welcome) · **No hay de qué** (don't mention it) · **Con mucho gusto** (with pleasure — Latin America).

sentences.base_language: You're welcome! Have a good trip.
sentences.grammarNote: 'Que + subjunctive' expresses a wish: 'Que tenga buen viaje' = Have a good trip (I hope you have…). Other common expressions: '¡Que tengas suerte!' (Good luck!), '¡Que te mejores!' (Get well soon!), '¡Que disfrutes!' (Enjoy!).
